narrative:
Pablo Jose Hernandez Rivera is the resident commissioner of Puerto Rico, which is Congress's way of saying "delegate" but with extra syllables and a four-year term instead of two. A Democrat who caucuses with the party on the federal level, he represents 3.2 million American citizens who cannot vote for president and whose representative cannot vote on final passage of bills [1]. Democracy is fun like that.

At 33, Hernandez is the youngest person ever to hold the resident commissioner post and the grandson of former Puerto Rico Governor Rafael Hernandez Colon [2]. He graduated from Harvard College and Stanford Law School, clerked for a federal judge in San Juan, then worked at a D.C. law firm and a major tech company before deciding that the best use of his pedigree was to return home and run for a job where he technically cannot vote [3]. He ran under the local Popular Democratic Party, which opposes statehood, and wrote an op-ed in The Hill announcing that Puerto Rican voters had chosen -- for the first time since 2000 -- someone who would "put statehood aside" and focus on economic development instead [4].

His legislative priorities center on federal parity: extending SNAP nutrition benefits, equalizing Medicaid funding, expanding the Child Tax Credit for Puerto Rican families (H.R. 1697), and strengthening supply-chain reshoring through bipartisan legislation co-introduced with Republican Rep. Nicole Malliotakis (H.R. 1328) [5]. He also introduced the Puerto Rico Postal Equity Act (H.R. 6441) and the Puerto Rico Water Infrastructure Resilience Act (H.R. 7397) [6]. His GovTrack absence rate of 9.8% -- roughly five times the House median -- has raised eyebrows, though defenders note the brutal San Juan-to-D.C. commute [7].

On the 80% Bill platform, Hernandez occupies an awkward constitutional limbo. He can vote in committee and in non-decisive floor votes, but his support for any of the twenty articles is largely symbolic. His OpenSecrets profile shows modest fundraising, with PAC support from the CHC BOLD PAC and standard Democratic donor networks [8]. Whether a young, Harvard-Stanford credentialed grandson of a governor who chose to represent a disenfranchised territory is an act of public service or an elaborate stepping stone remains, as they say, to be determined.

Jon Husted did not win his Senate seat -- he was handed it. When JD Vance vacated Ohio's Senate seat to become Vice President in January 2025, Governor Mike DeWine appointed his own Lieutenant Governor to fill the vacancy, skipping over a competitive process entirely [1]. Husted, a career Ohio politician who served as Speaker of the Ohio House and then Secretary of State before becoming Lt. Governor, arrived in Washington with no federal voting record but a long paper trail of state-level decisions that tell you everything you need to know [2].

As Ohio Secretary of State from 2011 to 2019, Husted became nationally known for aggressive voter roll purges. Under his watch, Ohio removed hundreds of thousands of voters from the rolls, disproportionately affecting minority and low-income communities. The practice was so aggressive it went to the Supreme Court in Husted v. A. Philip Randolph Institute (2018), where the conservative majority upheld Ohio's purge system in a 5-4 decision [3]. Husted did not just defend voter purges -- he championed them as a model for other states [4].

On the donor front, Husted's gubernatorial campaigns were bankrolled by Ohio's corporate establishment: Marathon Petroleum, FirstEnergy (the utility at the center of Ohio's massive HB 6 bribery scandal), and the Ohio Chamber of Commerce [5]. He has no OpenSecrets federal profile yet because he has never run a federal campaign -- he was simply appointed. His state-level fundraising history, however, shows deep ties to the fossil fuel and utility industries that dominate Ohio politics [6].

Since arriving in the Senate, Husted has aligned himself squarely with Republican leadership, opposing Democratic priorities across the board. He has shown no interest in banning congressional stock trading, corporate PACs, or dark money. He has not cosponsored any prescription drug pricing legislation, any voting rights restoration bill, or any Pentagon audit measure [7]. Given his history of voter suppression as Secretary of State, his opposition to the Freedom to Vote Act and the John Lewis Voting Rights Act is entirely predictable [8]. Husted is the revolving door in its purest form: a party insider elevated without a single vote cast by the people of Ohio, now representing 11.8 million Ohioans who never chose him [9].

Darrell Issa has served in Congress for a quarter century, and in March 2026 he announced he would not seek re-election from California's 48th District — ending a career that made him, at various points, the richest member of Congress [1]. Before politics, Issa built a $300 million fortune through Directed Electronics, the company behind the Viper car alarm. If you have ever heard a disembodied voice in a parking lot intone "please step away from the car," that was Issa — literally, his voice was the one recorded for the system [2]. He chaired the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee from 2011 to 2015, issuing over 100 subpoenas during the Obama years and positioning himself as Washington's top watchdog [3]. The irony is that a man who made his career demanding government accountability has a complicated relationship with accountability when it comes to his own finances.

On the 80% Bill, Issa is a study in selective populism. He introduced the SOIL Act to ban adversarial nations from purchasing American farmland near sensitive sites — a genuine alignment with the bill's farmland protections [4]. He co-authored the bipartisan SMART Act to shorten design patents on auto repair parts, a meaningful win for right-to-repair advocates and one of the few issues where Issa's personal expertise (37 patents to his name) and the public interest genuinely converge [5]. He even broke with his own party to vote against the 2017 Trump tax bill, citing its impact on California families through the SALT deduction cap — a vote that earned him rare bipartisan respect, though it conveniently aligned with the interests of his wealthy coastal constituents [6]. But these islands of agreement are surrounded by an ocean of opposition. Issa has opposed Medicare drug price negotiation, calling proposed reforms "draconian price controls" [7]. He has shown no enthusiasm for banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, overturning Citizens United, establishing paid family leave, or imposing congressional term limits — even as he voluntarily exits after 25 years in office.

Then there is the money. Issa disclosed up to $175 million in U.S. Treasury bond sales roughly 500 days late, in stark violation of the STOCK Act's 45-day reporting requirement. A day later, he filed another batch worth up to $180 million, also late [8]. The penalty for this kind of opacity? A $200 fine — the financial equivalent of a parking ticket for a man whose assets increased by at least $135 million in a single year [9]. His campaign has drawn contributions from defense contractors, real estate interests, and the electronics industry, sectors where his committee assignments and personal investments create overlapping interests [10]. The Center for Public Integrity flagged that Issa repeatedly used his Oversight Committee position to intervene in dealings with Goldman Sachs, Merrill Lynch, and DEI Holdings — all entities he was personally associated with [3].

The bottom line on Issa is that he is a man who genuinely believes in government accountability — for other people. He will investigate waste at the Pentagon but will not push to audit it. He will champion your right to repair your car but not your right to know who funds his colleagues' campaigns. He aligns with perhaps three or four of the 80% Bill's twenty provisions, and even those alignments come wrapped in caveats. As he prepares to leave Congress, his legacy is less "draining the swamp" and more "installing a very expensive alarm system on it."

Glenn Ivey's path to Congress is a case study in how the Democratic establishment manufactures its preferred candidates. A former Prince George's County State's Attorney and Harvard Law graduate, Ivey spent years as a federal prosecutor, Senate counsel, and corporate attorney at firms like Venable LLP before deciding that the revolving door between law, lobbying, and legislating needed one more spin [1]. He won his 2022 primary against progressive former Rep. Donna Edwards with critical assistance from AIPAC's United Democracy Project super PAC, which dumped nearly $6 million into the race — none of it mentioning Israel — to ensure the more corporate-friendly candidate prevailed [2]. Over half of Ivey's own fundraising that cycle came from AIPAC-connected donors, a fact that raises the question of whether his constituents in Maryland's 4th District elected a representative or adopted a lobbyist's preferred investment [3].

On the 80% Bill's twenty priorities, Ivey presents the familiar profile of a Democrat who agrees with popular policy in theory while doing little to champion it in practice. He has not co-sponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, impose term limits, or eliminate corporate PAC spending — positions that poll north of 70% with voters of both parties but remain conveniently absent from his legislative agenda [4]. His silence on the lobbying ban is particularly ironic given that LegiStorm records indicate he was himself registered as a lobbyist or foreign agent before entering Congress, a biographical detail his official website declines to emphasize [5]. When it comes to taxing the ultra-wealthy or closing the carried-interest loophole, Ivey's Appropriations Committee seat gives him real leverage, but his donor base of corporate law firms and PAC networks suggests that leverage will remain largely theoretical.

To his credit, Ivey has been active on criminal justice reform, drawing on his prosecutorial background to sponsor the Raise the Age Act and advocate for rehabilitative justice — an arena where his experience is genuine and his record is solid [6]. His work creating Prince George's County's Economic Crimes Unit, which became a national model for prosecuting mortgage fraud, earned legitimate praise. He also supported police body camera mandates and community policing initiatives, positions that align with several planks of the 80% Bill. His attendance record is strong, missing only 1.2% of votes, which at least demonstrates he shows up for work even when the work itself is incremental [7].

Yet the gap between Ivey's brand and his legislative ambition remains conspicuous. He sits on the Appropriations Committee, one of the most powerful perches in the House, yet has introduced no major legislation on Pentagon auditing, dark money disclosure, or corporate home-buying bans — all issues where his committee jurisdiction is directly relevant [8]. His voting record tracks almost perfectly with the Democratic median, which is another way of saying he rarely leads and never dissents. In a district that is safely blue and hungry for bold policy, Ivey has chosen the path of cautious institutionalism, raising the uncomfortable possibility that the millions AIPAC spent to install him over a progressive challenger purchased exactly the kind of legislator they wanted: present, pleasant, and unlikely to rock any boats that his donors have booked passage on.

Henry C. "Hank" Johnson, Jr. has represented Georgia's 4th Congressional District since 2007, when he unseated the firebrand incumbent Cynthia McKinney in a Democratic primary runoff by running as the calm, measured alternative [1]. A practicing attorney for over twenty-five years in DeKalb County and one of only a handful of Buddhists ever to serve in Congress, Johnson brought a quiet seriousness to the House -- though he is perhaps still best remembered nationally for a 2010 Armed Services Committee hearing in which he appeared to suggest the island of Guam might "tip over and capsize" from overpopulation, a remark his office later insisted was deadpan metaphor [2]. Whether or not the joke landed, Johnson has spent nearly two decades on the Judiciary Committee building a legislative record that, on the 80% Bill's terms, is one of the more aligned in the Georgia delegation.

On the issues that define the 80% Bill, Johnson checks a substantial number of boxes. He has publicly stated that stock trading by members of Congress is "unacceptable" and supported efforts to restrict it [3]. He is an original cosponsor of legislation to get secret corporate money out of politics and has called the Citizens United ruling a "dangerous precedent" that opened the floodgates for unlimited, undisclosed corporate spending [4]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation, capped insulin costs at $35 per month, and limited out-of-pocket expenses for seniors [5]. He has earned a perfect score from the Alliance for Retired Americans for his work protecting Social Security and Medicare from cuts [6]. On criminal justice, Johnson authored the Federal Police Camera and Accountability Act to require body cameras for federal law enforcement, and he has been one of the Judiciary Committee's most vocal advocates for the Justice in Policing Act [7].

Johnson's support extends to anti-gerrymandering measures and election security -- he cosponsored the For the People Act's anti-corruption provisions and introduced legislation to designate election infrastructure as critical under the Department of Homeland Security [8]. He supported the Build Back Better Act, which included paid family leave provisions, and has consistently backed legislation to strengthen Social Security funding rather than cut benefits. Where his record thins out is on issues like right to repair, farmland protection from foreign purchasers, and the carried interest loophole, where he has not been a visible champion, though he has generally voted with the Democratic caucus on tax fairness measures.

The money side of Johnson's ledger is relatively modest by congressional standards. His top contributing industries have historically included lawyers and law firms, which tracks with his professional background, along with contributions from labor unions and ideological groups aligned with the Democratic Party [9]. He does not appear on the lists of Congress's most prolific stock traders, and his financial disclosures are unremarkable compared to colleagues who have turned their committee seats into trading desks. That said, his reliance on PAC contributions from legal and lobbying interests -- the very ecosystem the 80% Bill seeks to constrain -- is a tension worth noting for a member who vocally opposes the influence of money in politics.

The bottom line on Hank Johnson is that he is a congressman whose voting record genuinely aligns with a large share of the 80% Bill's agenda, particularly on campaign finance transparency, Medicare drug pricing, police accountability, and Social Security. He is not a grandstander; he is the kind of workhorse Democrat who cosponsors the right bills and votes the right way without generating many headlines. Whether that low profile is Buddhist equanimity or simply the luxury of a safe blue district is a question only his constituents can answer.

Jim Jordan has represented Ohio's 4th Congressional District since 2007, a sprawling rural stretch from Elyria to Urbana that he has held with comfortable margins for nearly two decades [1]. A former NCAA wrestling champion at the University of Wisconsin, Jordan co-founded the House Freedom Caucus in 2015 and has built his reputation as one of the most combative conservatives on Capitol Hill [2]. He currently chairs the House Judiciary Committee, a perch he uses primarily to investigate the Biden administration, the FBI, and what he calls the "weaponization of the federal government" [3].

On the 80% Bill's twenty provisions, Jordan's record is a masterclass in populist rhetoric paired with legislative inaction. He has voiced support for congressional term limits and even cosponsored a term limits amendment [4], but on the financial transparency items that would actually constrain members of Congress, he has been conspicuously absent. He has not cosponsored the TRUST in Congress Act to ban congressional stock trading [5], nor has he backed the lifetime lobbying ban or the DISCLOSE Act. He voted against the Freedom to Vote Act and the John Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act, calling them federal overreach [6]. He opposed the Inflation Reduction Act, which contained the Medicare drug price negotiation provisions that 80% of Americans support [7]. He has never cosponsored legislation to close the carried interest loophole or to tax the ultra-wealthy, and he voted against the Junk Fee Prevention Act [8].

Where Jordan does overlap with the 80% Bill is revealing. He has supported efforts to audit the Pentagon and has questioned defense spending waste [9]. He voted for the repeal of the 2002 Iraq AUMF, aligning with the libertarian streak of the Freedom Caucus on war powers [10]. He has backed legislation to protect U.S. farmland from foreign adversaries [11]. And he has expressed support for releasing the Epstein files, a position that costs nothing and earns populist points [12].

Follow the money and the silences make more sense. Jordan's top industry donors include insurance companies, real estate interests, and the securities and investment sector [13]. His campaign has received significant contributions from PACs affiliated with Koch Industries and the Club for Growth [14]. He has raised over $24 million across his career, much of it from the very industries that benefit from the tax loopholes and deregulatory posture he defends. He has accepted corporate PAC money while publicly railing against the Washington establishment, a contradiction he has never been pressed to reconcile [15].

Jordan is a man who has made a career out of yelling at witnesses in committee hearings while quietly doing nothing about the policies that 80% of his own constituents support. He will fight to the death over cable-news controversies but cannot find the energy to cosponsor a bill banning congressional stock trading. His record tells a simple story: the fight is the product, and the money keeps the show running [16].

Ron Johnson ran for Senate in 2010 as a self-funding plastics manufacturer from Oshkosh who promised to bring business sense to Washington and — crucially — to limit himself to two terms. Twelve years later, he broke that promise with the casual ease of a man who has never met a principle he couldn't discard when it became inconvenient. When pressed, Johnson explained he was "just one of those truth-tellers," which is a fascinating way to describe someone who lied about term limits.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Johnson is a near-perfect foil. Ban stock trading? Johnson, worth an estimated $39 million, told reporters that a stock trading ban would make it "unattractive" to serve in Congress — apparently unaware that most Americans consider that a feature, not a bug. Fund Social Security? Johnson proposed converting Social Security and Medicare from guaranteed entitlements into discretionary spending subject to annual congressional approval, which is the legislative equivalent of telling your grandmother her rent is now month-to-month. The Washington Post reported that Johnson suggested ending Medicare and Social Security as mandatory spending programs entirely. Tax the ultra-wealthy? Johnson helped pass the 2017 Trump tax cuts that overwhelmingly benefited the donor class, including a last-minute provision benefiting pass-through businesses like, coincidentally, his own former company Pacur.

Then there's January 6th. Johnson's office attempted to hand-deliver fake elector slates from Wisconsin to Vice President Pence minutes before the certification vote. When confronted with text messages proving his staff's involvement, Johnson insisted his role "lasted seconds" — the political equivalent of claiming you only robbed the bank a little. CNN reported his campaign paid a law firm associated with the fake elector scheme for "recount consulting."

Johnson has also distinguished himself as the Senate's premier amplifier of conspiracy theories. He called climate change "bullshit," promoted ivermectin and hydroxychloroquine as COVID treatments, suggested the pandemic was "pre-planned" by unnamed elites, and in April 2025, floated 9/11 controlled demolition theories. The New York Times dubbed him the Republican Party's "foremost amplifier of conspiracy theories and disinformation," a title he wears like a badge of honor.

His top donors read like a who's who of billionaire influence: shipping magnate Elizabeth Uihlein gave $3.5 million to groups backing his 2022 race, while outside spending on his behalf topped $76 million. For a man who promised to be a citizen legislator, Johnson has become the embodiment of everything the 80% Bill exists to fix — a wealthy incumbent bankrolled by wealthier patrons, hostile to transparency, allergic to accountability, and perfectly comfortable undermining democratic institutions when it suits his party.

Hakeem Jeffries has spent over a decade in Congress perfecting the art of saying the right thing while doing the convenient thing. As House Democratic Leader, he commands one of the largest leadership PACs in American politics and has raised over $38 million across seven federal campaigns — a sum that buys a lot of "for the people" bumper stickers.

On banning congressional stock trading, Jeffries delivered a masterclass in political sabotage disguised as ambition. When a bipartisan discharge petition had enough votes to actually pass a stock trading ban in the House in late 2025, Jeffries actively discouraged Democrats from signing it. Instead, his team introduced a competing petition that extended the ban to the president and vice president — a provision they knew no Republican would support, effectively killing both efforts. He told advocates for the bipartisan bill to "get in line or risk losing access." The man who says members "cannot trade stock, period, full stop" apparently has a few more sentences after that full stop.

Jeffries co-sponsored the For the People Act and introduced the bipartisan JACK Act to require lobbyist criminal disclosure — solid reformist credentials on paper. But his top donors tell a different story. AIPAC has bundled over $900,000 for his campaigns. Employees of Paul Weiss, his former law firm, have given over $300,000. Apollo Global Management, one of the world's largest private equity firms, has contributed nearly $200,000. For a leader who claims to fight for working families, his donor rolodex reads like a Wall Street holiday party guest list.

On Medicare drug negotiation, Jeffries enthusiastically championed the Inflation Reduction Act and its prescription drug provisions — a genuine win. But on term limits, he opposes them outright, arguing that biennial elections are accountability enough. After 13 years in Congress and counting, one can see why he finds the current arrangement satisfactory.

The fundamental Jeffries paradox is this: he holds popular positions on most of the 80% Bill's planks — campaign finance reform, drug pricing, lobbying transparency — while simultaneously operating as the chief fundraiser and gatekeeper of a Democratic establishment that depends on the very systems those reforms would dismantle. He'll champion the cause right up until it might actually succeed, at which point he'll introduce a slightly different version that conveniently can't pass. It's not hypocrisy — it's strategy. And it's exactly why Congress has a 20% approval rating while its leaders keep getting re-elected.

David Joyce has represented Ohio's 14th Congressional District since 2013, covering the northeastern corner of the state from the Cleveland suburbs through Lake County to Ashtabula along the Lake Erie shore [1]. A former Geauga County prosecutor, Joyce is one of the quieter members of the Republican conference, a self-described pragmatist who sits on the powerful House Appropriations Committee [2]. He co-chairs the bipartisan Cannabis Caucus and has been one of the few Republicans to push for marijuana reform, a stance that occasionally puts him at odds with his party's leadership [3].

On the 80% Bill, Joyce occupies an unusual middle ground that manages to satisfy almost nobody. He voted for the bipartisan repeal of the 2002 Iraq AUMF, one of the easier votes on the list [4]. He has expressed support for protecting U.S. farmland from foreign adversaries and cosponsored related legislation [5]. He backed the REPAIR Act for right to repair on automobiles [6]. And he voted for the VA MISSION Act expansion that improved veterans' access to private healthcare [7]. But on the provisions that would rattle his donor base, Joyce goes quiet. He has not cosponsored the ban on congressional stock trading, despite the issue polling above 80% with voters of both parties [8]. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act and its Medicare drug negotiation provisions [9]. He has not backed the DISCLOSE Act, the corporate PAC ban, or the carried interest loophole fix [10].

Joyce's record on voting rights and democratic reform is particularly instructive. He voted against the Freedom to Vote Act and the John Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act [11]. He has not cosponsored the constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United [12]. On paid family leave, he voted against the FAMILY Act while offering no Republican alternative [13]. He has been silent on the proposal to ban corporate purchases of single-family homes, despite his district containing communities where institutional investors have reshaped the housing market [14].

The donor ledger explains the gaps. Joyce's top industry contributors include real estate, insurance, health professionals, and the securities and investment sector [15]. He has received significant contributions from pharmaceutical and health product PACs, which may illuminate his opposition to expanded drug price negotiation [16]. His campaigns have collected well over $10 million in career contributions, with corporate PACs making up a substantial share of his fundraising [17]. The National Association of Realtors and the American Bankers Association are among his reliable backers, industries that have a direct interest in killing the corporate home-buying ban and the carried interest fix [18].

Joyce is the kind of representative who benefits most from voter inattention. He is not loud enough to draw scrutiny and not bold enough to earn credit. On the handful of 80% Bill items that carry no financial cost to his donors, he is happy to vote yes. On the rest, he simply does not show up. It is a strategy that works until someone starts keeping score [19].

Pramila Jayapal is the rare member of Congress who actually appears to have read the 80% Bill's platform and thought, "Yes, all of that." As chair of the Congressional Progressive Caucus, she has introduced or co-sponsored legislation touching nearly every plank: a constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United, a bill to abolish Super PACs, the Ultra-Millionaire Tax Act, Medicare for All, and paid family leave provisions in the Build Back Better framework. If the 80% Bill had a congressional caucus, Jayapal would be angling for the gavel.

Her campaign finance record backs up the rhetoric, at least compared to her colleagues. Jayapal is one of only four returning Progressive Caucus members who have pledged to reject corporate PAC money. Her 2023-2024 fundraising drew 58.82% from large individual contributions, 26.23% from small donors, and just 14.95% from PACs. Her top donor was Microsoft at $42,012, which is less a corporate capture story and more a reflection of representing a district where half the neighborhood works in Redmond. In a Congress where AIPAC and defense contractors regularly top donor lists, Jayapal's fundraising profile is almost suspiciously clean.

On campaign finance reform, Jayapal has been a legislative machine. Her We the People Amendment would establish that constitutional rights belong to natural persons, not corporations, and that money is not speech. In 2025, she introduced this amendment again, explicitly calling out Elon Musk's role as a "Shadow President" bankrolling political influence. She followed that up weeks later by co-introducing the Abolish Super PACs Act with Ro Khanna and Summer Lee, capping super PAC contributions at $5,000 per year. She has also pushed for the DISCLOSE Act to end dark money in elections.

On economic policy, the Ultra-Millionaire Tax Act she co-introduced with Elizabeth Warren would levy a 2% annual tax on wealth above $50 million, projected to generate $3 trillion over a decade. She has championed Medicare for All since arriving in Congress, founding both the Medicare for All Caucus and the Medicare for All PAC. She fought to include paid family leave and Medicare expansion for dental, vision, and hearing in the Build Back Better framework.

The knock on Jayapal is that she represents a D+35 district in Seattle, which makes bold progressive positions less a profile in courage and more a job requirement. Fair enough. But in a body where most members use their safe seats to coast and cash checks from lobbyists, Jayapal at least uses hers to introduce the legislation that 80% of Americans say they want. Whether any of it passes is another question entirely, but you cannot accuse her of not trying.
